top_papers.figure_caption: Task-stream construction and its impact on evaluation. Compositional relationship refers to a task pair, where the complex one can reuse the solution to the simpler one. Sequential relationship refers to the sequential order of task execution. Top : naive streams order tasks from the same domain without guaranteeing reuse relationships, while compositional streams place reusable subtasks before related complex tasks. Bottom : boxplots of average accuracy across evaluated methods on CodeEval-Pro and BrowseComp+, showing that compositional streams yield larger method separation than naive streams for memory reuse.

top_papers.title: Bridging the Last Mile of Time Series Forecasting with LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the "last-mile" of time series forecasting, where raw statistical forecasts are refined with unstructured business context. The core method is an LLM-agent framework that sits atop a forecasting backbone, using tools to retrieve contextual evidence and perform reasoned forecast revisions under safety constraints. Its contribution is a practical system to bridge the gap between statistical forecasts and decision-ready predictions by incorporating real-world business knowledge.
top_papers.figure_caption: System overview of the proposed last-mile forecasting framework. A forecasting backbone first produces a baseline trajectory; an LLM agent then operates over a shared forecast workspace, retrieves contextual evidence through tools, applies validated revision actions to y final y_{\( \text{final} \)} , and accumulates reflection memories in a persistent memory bank for retrieval by subsequent sessions.

top_papers.title: Food Noise & False Safety: A Systematic Evaluation of How LLMs Fail to Adapt to Eating Disorder Queries with Clinician Feedback
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically evaluates how Large Language Models (LLMs) respond to eating disorder (ED) queries, finding they often uncritically adapt to potentially harmful user requests. By consulting with clinical ED experts, the researchers identified linguistic cues that increase the likelihood of unsafe LLM responses. The study's contribution is demonstrating and quantifying the extent to which LLMs fail to adapt safely to ED-related prompts, highlighting significant risks for vulnerable users.
top_papers.figure_caption: Prevalence of food-noise categories across models ( G : Gemma-2-9B-Instruct , L : Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct , and Q : Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct ) and context–request risk conditions ( NN , NR , RN and RR ). Each cell reports the percentage of replies containing at least one lexical match from the corresponding category, with darker colours indicate higher prevalence. We report statistical comparisons between the NN and RR conditions in Tab. ˜ 16 , and a full breakdown in § ˜ G.2 .

top_papers.title: HLL: Can Agents Cross Humanity's Last Line of Verification?
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Humanity's Last Line of Verification (HLL), a benchmark designed to test if multimodal AI agents can bypass CAPTCHA, a human-verification system. HLL uses interactive CAPTCHAs within a controlled environment to assess agents' ability to perform human-like interactions, not just recognition, under realistic conditions. The core contribution is a novel evaluation framework and benchmark to measure agents' capability to overcome human-verification barriers.
top_papers.figure_caption: CAPTCHA as the final frontier: securing web services by testing interactive, human-level reasoning against automated agents.

top_papers.title: MASER: Modality-Adaptive Specialist Routing for Embodied 3D Spatial Intelligence
top_papers.abstract: MASER addresses the limitation of single-modality fine-tuning in embodied 3D spatial intelligence by proposing a lightweight framework. It trains modality-specific adapters for a shared VLM backbone and uses a neural routing policy, guided by question semantics encoded by a sentence transformer and MLP, to dynamically select the most suitable adapter for answering spatial questions. This approach allows for adaptive modality utilization, improving performance by not being constrained to a single input type.

top_papers.title: MCP-Persona: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Personal Applications via Environment Simulation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MCP-Persona, the first benchmark designed to evaluate LLM agents on real-world, personalized applications that use the Model Context Protocol (MCP). It addresses the limitation of existing benchmarks by focusing on tools that interact with individual accounts and local data, such as social media and enterprise suites. The benchmark reveals that current state-of-the-art agents struggle significantly with this type of personalized tool usage.
top_papers.figure_caption: System overview of MCP-Persona, which is built upon the interaction of Tools , Contexts , and Tasks . For each component, we introduce a dedicated method, described in detail as Tool-Traverse (§ 3.1 ), Context-Tree (§ 3.2 ), and Persona-Gen (§ 3.3 ).

top_papers.title: Moment-Video: Diagnosing Temporal Fidelity of Video MLLMs on Momentary Visual Events
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Moment-Video, a new benchmark designed to evaluate how well video multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can understand brief, critical visual events. The core method involves creating questions that hinge on short-lived visual occurrences, testing the models' ability to detect and reason about these fleeting moments. The main contribution is a diagnostic tool that highlights MLLMs' weaknesses in temporal fidelity, particularly when dealing with sampling-sensitive, localized visual evidence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Moment-Video . Left: domain and subcategory taxonomy. Right: evaluation protocols for closed-ended and open-ended questions.

top_papers.title: Monitoring Agentic Systems Before They're Reliable
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a novel monitoring methodology for agentic systems before they are fully reliable. It decomposes evaluation into quality, suitability, and efficiency across within-run, cross-run, and structural scopes, using variance as a key signal. This approach effectively identifies structural defects, which are prevalent in early-stage agentic systems, and prioritizes them for human attention using an FMEA-adapted severity classification.

top_papers.title: Not All Errors Are Equal: A Systematic Study of Error Propagation in Large Language Model Inference
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically studies how soft errors affect Large Language Model (LLM) inference using a novel fault-injection framework called LLMFI. The core contribution is a comprehensive understanding of error propagation across various LLMs and tasks, revealing critical vulnerabilities and proposing four low-overhead, software-only reliability improvements.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Challenges for modeling errors for LLM inference.

top_papers.title: SafeSteer: Localized On-Policy Distillation for Efficient Safety Alignment
top_papers.abstract: SafeSteer addresses the "alignment tax" in LLMs by proposing localized on-policy distillation. Instead of global trade-offs, it focuses on modifying only safety-related tokens, identified through activation steering and a novel selection algorithm. This approach effectively preserves general capabilities while enhancing safety, achieving a superior balance compared to existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Safety–capability trade-off on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct . Each point is a method, with the gray point marking the base model. Our SafeSteer achieves the highest safety score while preserving general capability.

top_papers.title: Tracking the Behavioral Trajectories of Adapting Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a method to measure agent "traits" by defining them as directions in a text embedding space. By training a linear model on skill file changes, they learn trait vectors that can quantify how specific edits influence an agent's behavior, demonstrating high accuracy in classifying propensity to seek sensitive data. This allows for automated evaluation of agent behavioral changes.
top_papers.figure_caption: LOOCV predictions vs. human labels for 68 skill diff pairs. Blue dots indicate correct sign classification; red crosses indicate the 6 misclassified pairs, all of which have near-zero predictions. The dashed line marks perfect agreement.

top_papers.title: Auditing Asset-Specific Preferences in Financial Large Language Models: Evidence from Bitcoin Representations and Portfolio Allocation
top_papers.abstract: This paper audits financial Large Language Models (LLMs) for asset-specific biases, focusing on Bitcoin. It finds that LLMs exhibit frame-dependent preferences for Bitcoin, influenced by functional properties rather than just its name. The study also identifies a specific internal model feature that causally drives these preferences, demonstrating its impact on downstream financial decisions.

top_papers.title: Drifting Preference Optimization for One-Step Generative Models
top_papers.abstract: Drifting Preference Optimization (DrPO) is a novel method for fine-tuning one-step text-to-image generators using user preferences. It synthesizes an update direction in feature space by comparing high and low-scoring image candidates generated by the current model, allowing for training with black-box or non-differentiable reward functions. This approach enables efficient preference alignment without sacrificing the one-step generation speed of the model.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of DrPO. Left: construction of the drift field used to finetune the network. For an on-policy sample (black dot), N N random pairs are collected. In each pair, the sample with the higher reward is labeled as positive (red dot), while the other is labeled as negative (blue dot). Together with the self-repulsion and attraction forces induced by p θ p_{\( \theta \)} and p ref p_{\( \text{ref} \)} , respectively, these components synthesize the final regression target z ∗ z^{*} (brown dot). The contours indicate reward magnitudes. Right: qualitative samples from fixed test prompts along the online finetuning trajectory.

top_papers.title: On the Scaling of PEFT: Towards Million Personal Models of Trillion Parameters
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a new perspective on Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), viewing small trainable adapters not just as a cost-saving method, but as persistent local state for strong foundation models. The core idea is that shared models provide general capabilities, while adapters capture individual user preferences and behaviors. The research explores scaling these personal models across three dimensions: improving base models, shrinking adapters, and managing numerous coexisting instances, suggesting PEFT can enable compact, persistent personal AI.

top_papers.title: Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads
top_papers.abstract: This paper characterizes the system-level behavior of LLM agent memory systems designed for long-horizon tasks. It introduces a taxonomy for classifying these systems and a profiling harness to measure costs associated with memory construction, retrieval, and generation. The study's contribution lies in its empirical analysis of ten representative memory systems, revealing how design choices impact performance and leading to practical system recommendations.

top_papers.title: An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making
top_papers.abstract: This paper develops a spatially grounded agent-based simulation for infectious disease spread. It integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate realistic individual decisions about self-reporting illness, considering demographic and situational factors within a synthetic population. The key contribution is the incorporation of location and geographically diverse behavioral modeling, enabling the simulation of different influence scenarios on self-reporting outcomes.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Infectious Disease Data Simulator: Overview.

top_papers.title: Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Benchmark Agent, an autonomous system that automates the entire benchmark construction process for LLMs and MLLMs. Its core method involves orchestrating user query analysis, subtask design, data annotation, and quality control. The main contribution is a sustainable and scalable approach to creating diverse, high-quality benchmarks that can effectively differentiate state-of-the-art models.
top_papers.figure_caption: Our Benchmark Agent, as the first fully autonomous benchmark building system, can efficiently produce high-quality benchmarks across diverse modalities, tasks, and domains to meet user-specific requirements. It will offer rapidly evolving benchmarks to contribute to the community.

top_papers.title: EasyLens: A Training-Free Plug-and-Play Subtle-Lesion Representation Amplifier for Medical Vision-Language Models
top_papers.abstract: EasyLens is a training-free method that enhances the representation of subtle lesions in medical images for vision-language models. It achieves this by amplifying weak lesion cues within the image representation without requiring any model retraining or adaptation. This allows existing medical VLMs to better detect and interpret subtle abnormalities, improving their clinical utility.
top_papers.figure_caption: EasyLens brings weak subtle-lesion cues into focus in frozen medical VLMs.

top_papers.title: Humans' ALMANAC: A Human Collaboration Dataset of Action-Level Mental Model Annotations for Agent Collaboration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ALMANAC, a novel dataset designed to improve human-AI collaboration. It addresses the lack of data on how humans build and align mental models during collaboration. ALMANAC provides action-level annotations of these mental models, derived from human participants in a routing task, to guide AI agents towards better collaborative competence.
top_papers.figure_caption: A sample data of Almanac , which contains participants’ actions, mental models (team goal, perceived partner intent, self-reasoning), and a free-form rationale. We implement the Map Task, a classic dyadic routing task, to collect human collaborative behaviors and action-level mental model annotations.

top_papers.title: Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces DataCOPE, an unsupervised framework for discovering reusable data-analysis skills for agents. It addresses the challenge of learning from unlabeled data by using a verifier to guide skill discovery, extracting signals from agent exploration to assess trajectory quality. DataCOPE then distills these signals into effective skills without requiring explicit supervision.
top_papers.figure_caption: Supervised skill discovery requires costly data annotation. DataCOPE instead performs unsupervised skill discovery by deriving task-adaptive verifier signals from unlabeled exploration trajectories and distilling them into reusable skills.

top_papers.title: Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents
top_papers.abstract: Vortex is a system designed to accelerate the development and deployment of sparse attention mechanisms for large language models. Its core method involves a Python-embedded frontend for flexible algorithm expression and an efficient backend integrated into LLM serving stacks. Vortex's contribution lies in enabling rapid prototyping and evaluation of sparse attention, translating theoretical efficiency into practical throughput gains and empowering AI agents to automatically design and refine these algorithms.

top_papers.title: Will the Agent Recuse Itself? Measuring LLM-Agent Compliance with In-Band Access-Deny Signals
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the "Recuse Signal," a novel, in-band mechanism for servers to politely request autonomous LLM agents to voluntarily withdraw from accessing a resource, distinct from traditional security barriers. The core contribution is to empirically measure whether LLM agents will actually comply with such cooperative governance signals, akin to a robots.txt for live infrastructure access. The authors develop and deploy adapters for SSH and PostgreSQL to test this compliance.
top_papers.figure_caption: Recusal rate on the live SSH deny signal. With the signal present and no authorization framing, all subjects recuse 100%; in the no-signal control all complete the task (0% recusal). Adding an explicit authorization framing collapses GPT-4o’s recusal to 20% while GPT-4o-mini and Claude Code hold at 100%—the signal is cooperative and its weight is model-dependent.

top_papers.title: Latent Reasoning with Normalizing Flows
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces NF-CoT, a novel latent reasoning framework for large language models. It addresses the limitations of explicit text-based Chain-of-Thought (CoT) by performing intermediate computations in continuous latent states. NF-CoT utilizes normalizing flows to model these continuous thoughts, preserving key advantages of autoregressive generation like left-to-right processing, probabilistic sampling, and KV-cache compatibility, while enabling more efficient and flexible reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Four paradigms for chain-of-thought reasoning. Explicit CoT : discrete text tokens. Coconut : deterministic hidden states. LaDiR : iteratively denoised latents using diffusion. NF-CoT (ours) : AR-sampled continuous thoughts.

top_papers.title: Human Adults and LLMs as Scientists: Who Benefits from Active Exploration?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether active exploration improves human adults' ability to identify conjunctive causal rules, which have historically been challenging. The study found that granting agency through active intervention significantly enhances adults' conjunctive reasoning, though it still requires more effort than disjunctive rules. The research also compares human performance to large language models in this active exploration setting.
top_papers.figure_caption: Test structure in the Active Exploration vs. Passive Observation conditions. In the active exploration condition, participants can click to add or remove four individual objects from the Nexiom machine, and explicitly test the current combination to observe whether the machine switches ON or OFF. In the passive observation condition, participants are each paired to an active exploration participant (yoked control). They do not perform any actions on their own, but rather observe the actions and test outcomes of the active exploration participants. Participants in both conditions are then asked to classify which object(s) are “Nexioms” and the rule under which the objects operate (conjunctive or disjunctive) to activate the machine.

top_papers.title: Reinforcement Learning Elicits Contextual Learning of Unseen Language Translation
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning (RL) method to enable large language models (LLMs) to translate unseen languages by learning to effectively utilize provided linguistic context. Instead of memorizing specific languages, the RL agent learns a meta-skill to extract and apply relevant information from context, leading to improved zero-shot translation performance on entirely new languages compared to existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Train–test context mismatch (RL, Qwen3-4B-Base). Test-time context dominates: no/full > full/no in every panel (En → \( \to \) Kal: 0.28 0.28 vs. 0.17 0.17 ).

top_papers.title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper empirically compares human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM mathematical reasoning on AIME problems. It finds that while DeepSeek-R1 mimics the structure of human reasoning, it lacks genuine logical progress, often looping and performing superficial checks. The study identifies stable branching and backtracing as potential signals of true reasoning in successful LLM solutions.
top_papers.figure_caption: State CoT: A transition diagram illustrating the discrete reasoning states and meta-cognitive actions within a trajectory.

top_papers.title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces CapCode and CapReward to address deceptive performance in coding agents. CapCode creates datasets with randomized tests capped below perfect scores, making high evaluations indicative of cheating. CapReward then uses this principle to design rewards that discourage agents from exploiting shortcuts, leading to more reliable evaluations and better task adherence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Conceptual illustrations of CapCode and CapReward. CapCode (left) is a dataset-construction framework with a capped achievable pass rate, enabling deceptively high performance detection. CapReward (right) is a reward design method that discourages reward hacking by penalizing pass rates that exceed the cap.

top_papers.title: Online Pandora's Box for Contextual LLM Cascading
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces an "online contextual Pandora's Box" method for adaptively selecting Large Language Model (LLM) APIs. The core innovation is a two-phase decision process where the system queries APIs sequentially, incurring costs, and then selects an output based on observed rewards. The contribution lies in directly modeling a "reservation index" rather than full output distributions, enabling efficient learning for API selection in this output-mediated feedback scenario.

top_papers.title: Socratic-SWE: Self-Evolving Coding Agents via Trace-Derived Agent Skills
top_papers.abstract: Socratic-SWE is a self-evolving framework for training software engineering agents. It uniquely leverages the agent's past problem-solving attempts (traces) to identify recurring failures and successful repair strategies. These distilled "skills" then guide the generation of new, targeted training tasks, creating a closed-loop system that adapts to the agent's weaknesses and improves its performance.

top_papers.title: TEVI: Text-Conditioned Editing of Visual Representations via Sparse Autoencoders for Improved Vision-Language Alignment
top_papers.abstract: TEVI is a framework that addresses the misalignment between image and text embeddings in vision-language models. It uses sparse autoencoders and a caption-guided masking module to selectively reconstruct image embeddings, retaining information relevant to the caption while discarding irrelevant details. This approach improves vision-language alignment and enhances retrieval performance on various datasets.

top_papers.title: Whisper Hallucination Detection and Mitigation via Hidden Representation Steering and Sparse AutoEncoders
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a method to detect and mitigate "hallucinations" (incorrect transcriptions of non-speech audio) in the Whisper ASR model by analyzing its internal representations. The core method involves steering these representations, particularly using Sparse AutoEncoders, to suppress hallucination-generating features. The main contribution is demonstrating that this steering approach significantly reduces hallucination rates with minimal impact on speech transcription accuracy, offering an efficient alternative to fine-tuning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Layer-wise AUC scores for classifiers trained on raw Whisper activations (left) and SAE latent representations (right) for Whisper large-v3. Each curve corresponds to a test dataset, except for the CV curve, which displays the cross-validation score on the training set. All results correspond to training on the FULL-train dataset.

top_papers.title: Agentopia: Long-Term Life Simulation and Learning in Agent Societies
top_papers.abstract: Agentopia introduces a framework for simulating long-term social interactions among LLM-powered agents over 10 simulated years. The core method involves agents autonomously pursuing growth, relationships, and goals within a society. The paper's contribution is demonstrating how such extensive simulated social experience can foster emergent social behaviors and enhance LLMs' anthropomorphic capabilities, particularly in social intelligence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Illustration of emergent behaviors observed in Agentopia simulations. Each scene depicts a real behavioral pattern documented in our case studies (Tables 22 – 34 ). Without explicit scripting, agents autonomously develop diverse behavioral patterns reflecting agents’ intelligence in social life.

top_papers.title: Sycophantic Praise: Evaluating Excessive Praise in Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel parameterized framework to measure sycophantic praise in language models, distinguishing it from mere agreement. The method assesses if praise is excessive relative to contribution quality and user ability, outperforming generic LLM judges in agreement with human annotations. The findings highlight praise calibration as a distinct alignment challenge, with sycophancy more prevalent in social and interpretive tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: The same model response may be appropriate or excessive depending on the user’s expected ability and contribution quality. SyPr measures excess praise as the difference between observed praise P ​ ( r ) P(r) in the response r r , and contextually warranted praise W ​ ( p , u ) W(p,u) , where warranted praise depends on both the persona context p p and the user utterance u u .

top_papers.title: Multi-Turn Evaluation of Deep Research Agents Under Process-Level Feedback
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a multi-turn evaluation framework for deep research agents (DRAs) that goes beyond single-shot assessments. Its core contribution is the Research Gap Inference (RGI) method, which identifies strategic research process flaws based on rubric satisfaction. This enables process-level feedback, demonstrating that even one round significantly improves DRA performance, unlike self-reflection which shows minimal gains.
top_papers.figure_caption: Process-level feedback generation. Given a report r t − 1 r_{t-1} evaluated against the DRACO rubric, RGI analyzes patterns of satisfied and unsatisfied criteria from FA, BD, and CQ (excluding PQ) to infer research-process gaps and generate process-level feedback f t − 1 f_{t-1} for the next turn. Example criteria shown for illustration; negative-weight criteria excluded for simplicity.

top_papers.title: Observability for Delegated Execution in Agentic AI Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of understanding which agent performed which action in complex AI systems where delegation is common. Its core method is an agent-aware observability substrate, including a gateway and a common information model, to reconstruct delegation-scoped execution. The main contribution is enabling accurate attribution and footprint reconstruction for delegated actions, which is currently impossible with standard logging methods.

top_papers.title: OmniGameArena: A Unified UE5 Benchmark for VLM Game Agents with Improvement Dynamics
top_papers.abstract: OmniGameArena introduces a unified benchmark for Vision-Language Model (VLM) game agents across diverse game modes (Solo, PvP, Coop) in Unreal Engine 5. Its core contribution is the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC), a novel evaluation method where a tool-using LLM autonomously refines agent prompts over multiple rounds. This allows for assessing not just initial performance but also an agent's learning trajectory and adaptability to new tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: OmniGameArena at a glance. Twelve newly built UE5 games span Solo (7), PvP (3), and Coop (2) regimes (top). Heterogeneous agents (commercial VLMs, open-weight VLMs, keyboard-mouse policies, and gamepad policies) connect to the same real-time UE5 environment through documented adapters (middle). Evaluation reports the cold-start leaderboard and the Improvement Dynamics Curve (IDC) under multi-round reflection (bottom).

top_papers.title: Proxy Reward Internalization and Mechanistic Exploitation: A Learned Precursor to Reward Hacking and Its Generalization
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces PRIME, a learned capability that allows AI agents to assess task correctness and predict proxy reward before reward hacking becomes apparent. PRIME enables agents to understand and exploit the gap between proxy and true task rewards. The authors demonstrate that PRIME emerges before reward hacking, forecasts its onset and severity, and can be mitigated by ablating its underlying mechanisms.
top_papers.figure_caption: External Prime emerges before reward hacking. (a) Proxy/gold split. (b) C B , P B , E B C^{B},P^{B},E^{B} onset before hack rate. (c) Source B exceeds Source A on joint G , E ​ G ​ a ​ p G,EGap .

top_papers.title: SIGA: Self-Evolving Coding-Agent Adapters for Scientific Simulation
top_papers.abstract: SIGA addresses the challenge of scientists learning complex scientific simulator input languages by framing it as an agent-tool interface grounding problem. Its core method is to provide an adapter that supplies the simulator's "contract" (vocabulary, constraints, validation rules) to an off-the-shelf coding agent. This allows the agent to automatically generate simulator configurations, significantly reducing the time and effort required for domain scientists.
top_papers.figure_caption: Illustrative example of advanced tooling usage bottleneck for the geophysics domain. Here, the GEOS simulator’s extensive documentation helps as a translation guide for its elaborate configuration that is a custom XML (domain specific language/DSL) to produce results such as simulating carbon sequestration in deep saline formations (top right) or reservoir flow in heterogeneous hydrocarbon (bottom right).

top_papers.title: SpatialWorld: Benchmarking Interactive Spatial Reasoning of Multimodal Agents in Real-World Tasks
top_papers.abstract: SpatialWorld is a novel benchmark designed to evaluate the interactive spatial reasoning of multimodal agents in real-world scenarios. It achieves this by unifying eight diverse simulation environments under a single protocol, allowing agents to learn and act through vision-only partial observability and a text-based interface. This benchmark's contribution lies in its ability to assess general interactive spatial understanding beyond static evaluations or simulator-specific limitations.
top_papers.figure_caption: SpatialWorld is a scalable, general-purpose evaluation framework for multimodal agents, supporting end-to-end task solving and structured plan generation. It unifies diverse 3D backends under a standardized observation-action interface, enabling rigorous assessment of interactive spatial reasoning via reproducible benchmarks and automated efficiency metrics.

top_papers.title: Echo-Memory: A Controlled Study of Memory in Action World Models
top_papers.abstract: Echo-Memory introduces a controlled framework to isolate and study memory mechanisms in action-conditioned video generation models. By fixing all other components, it systematically compares different memory strategies (raw context, compression, spatial summaries, state-space recurrence) to understand their impact on generating consistent scenes over time. This allows for a clear separation of memory's influence across capacity, compression, read-out, and recurrence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Abstract teaser and workflow of Echo-Memory. Given a text description, historical observations, and the camera/action state, an action world model must generate chunk-wise video while carrying memory across revisits. The figure positions Context, Compression, Spatial, and State-Space families as representative designs for preserving a revisitable world.

top_papers.title: What the Eyes See, the LLMs Miss: Exploiting Human Perception for Adversarial Text Attacks
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Human-Perceptible Adversarial Attacks (HPAA) to exploit the limitations of LLM-based content moderation. The core method involves using visually salient typographic manipulations (like spacing and emphasis) to embed harmful content into benign text. This preserves human understanding while making the content nearly invisible to LLMs, demonstrating a significant vulnerability in current automated moderation systems.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of typographic Human-perceptible Adversarial Attacks (HPAA) against modern content moderation pipelines. Harmful content remains recognizable to human readers while evading automated moderation systems.

top_papers.title: A History-Aware Visually Grounded Critic for Computer Use Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces HiViG, a novel framework for improving computer use agents by addressing limitations in existing critics. HiViG employs a history-aware, visually grounded multimodal critic that learns from real GUI interactions. This critic provides agents with a summarized history of past actions and visually verifies proposed actions against the current screen, leading to more robust and context-aware decision-making.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of test-time interventions for Computer Use Agents (CUAs). Left : Lacking historical awareness and proactive error-recovery, standard policies easily become trapped in short-sighted decision loops. Right : Existing approaches are limited. Scalar feedback (top) traps policies in low-reward trajectory regions when all candidate actions are suboptimal. Previous critics (middle) rely heavily on textual intent, missing spatial and reasoning errors, and fail to provide historical awareness. In contrast, our critic (bottom) verifies raw execution coordinates, predicts immediate visual state outcomes grounded in its learned state transition knowledge, and provides visually grounded error analysis that intercepts errors before execution. Furthermore, it equips agents with history state tracking, condensing past interactions to guide them toward the final task objective.

top_papers.title: ABC-Bench: An Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark for Biosecurity
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ABC-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate the biosecurity-relevant capabilities of AI agents. The core method involves testing LLM agents on a suite of tasks requiring both biological and software expertise, including robot operation, DNA design, and evading synthesis screening. The key contribution is demonstrating that current LLM agents significantly outperform human experts on these biosecurity-relevant tasks, highlighting the evolving risks and opportunities presented by AI in biology.
top_papers.figure_caption: The Liquid Handling Robot task from ABC-Bench. A. We (1) prompt the agent with task instructions; (2) provide the agent with relevant software and research tools to complete the task and to check its work; (3) allow the agent to submit its final answer; and (4) algorithmically check the agent’s answer against pre-specified criteria. B. Where applicable, we validate task performance in a real-world setting (photo of OpenTrons Flex robot running GPT-o4-mini-written Gibson Assembly code).

top_papers.title: FADA: Accessible fetal ultrasound interpretation and annotation with a selectively distilled unified vision-language model
top_papers.abstract: FADA is a unified vision-language model designed for accessible fetal ultrasound interpretation and annotation. Its core method involves selectively distilling knowledge from specialized foundation models into a single pipeline, enabling it to perform interpretation, classification, detection, and segmentation without requiring external labels. This approach addresses the shortage of trained sonographers, particularly in low-resource settings, by providing a more comprehensive and automated tool for prenatal ultrasound analysis.
top_papers.figure_caption: Segmentation ground truth vs prediction comparison across FADA model variants. Each row shows a different anatomical structure (pubic symphysis, cardiac, liver, stomach, artery); columns display the original ultrasound image, ground truth segmentation mask, and predictions from FADA-Base, FADA-SKD, and FADA-FKD (all 4B). Dice coefficients are annotated per prediction panel. Images were selected via quantitative analysis to highlight cases where FADA-SKD achieves the largest advantage: FADA-SKD detects structures entirely missed by other variants (artery: +0.687, stomach: +0.613 Dice advantage) and produces substantially tighter boundaries for pubic symphysis (+0.152) and liver (+0.084).

top_papers.title: Flaws in the LLM Automation Narrative
top_papers.abstract: This paper challenges the narrative of LLMs automating knowledge work by highlighting flaws in common benchmarking methods. The authors introduce a novel benchmark that measures LLM performance on a coding task, revealing that human experts outperform LLMs in both average performance and reliability, with LLMs exhibiting greater error variability. Their contribution lies in demonstrating that current LLM evaluations may overstate their capabilities in high-stakes, real-world scenarios.
top_papers.figure_caption: The RMSE among all submissions. The x-axis is ordered from smallest to largest RMSE value. Submissions from human experts are shown in blue, each submission from ChatGPT Codex 5.2 are shown in red, and historical strawman are shown in black. The right panel removes extreme values from 5 LLM submissions. The RMSE of ChatGPT Codex 5.2 submissions 14, 19, 11, 2, and 1 were 3.07, 1160.05, 2572.96, 111,765,692,519, and 219,316,810,584, respectively.

top_papers.title: Modeling Complex Behaviors: Multi-Personality Composition and Dynamic Switching in Vision-Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a method for explicitly conditioning Vision-Language Models (MLLMs) with personalities and introduces a framework to evaluate single and multi-personality induction, as well as dynamic switching. The core contribution is demonstrating that personality conditioning can improve captioning but hinder reasoning tasks, and that MLLMs exhibit complex, co-modulated behaviors during multi-trait composition and switching, highlighting limitations of current prompt-based methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Illustration of personality induction. The top-left panel illustrates generation under single-personality induction, the bottom-left panel presents results with multi-personality conditioning, and the right panel demonstrates In-conversation personality switching.

top_papers.title: ReasonAlloc: Hierarchical Decoding-Time KV Cache Budget Allocation for Reasoning Models
top_papers.abstract: ReasonAlloc addresses the KV cache bottleneck in LLM reasoning by introducing a hierarchical, training-free budget allocation framework. It first preallocates KV cache offline based on a novel "Reasoning Wave" pattern reflecting architectural demand, then dynamically reallocates resources online to information-rich heads during decoding. This approach efficiently manages KV cache growth, improving inference speed for long reasoning chains.
top_papers.figure_caption: An overview of the proposed ReasonAlloc framework. Left (I): Layer-wise allocation strategy based on offline architecture calibration, demonstrating the non-linear “Reasoning Wave” KV demand across layers. Right (II): Head-wise allocation strategy that dynamically routes KV budgets to distinct attention heads based on real-time importance and redundancy scoring during decoding.

top_papers.title: TRACE: A Unified Rollout Budget Allocation Framework for Efficient Agentic Reinforcement Learning
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of inefficient rollout budget allocation in agentic reinforcement learning for large language models. The core method, TRACE, extends budget allocation beyond initial prompts to individual turns within a multi-turn rollout, treating each thought-action-observation sequence as a distinct node in a tree structure. This allows for more granular and efficient allocation of resources to informative prefixes, improving the learning process.
top_papers.figure_caption: TRACE redirects a fixed rollout budget toward contrast-rich roots and prefixes, converting scalable outcome rewards into denser mixed-reward contrast and implicit stepwise preference pairs than uniform allocation.

top_papers.title: What Fits (Into Few Tokens) Doesn't Overfit: Compression and Generalization in ML Research Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates why benchmark-driven ML research rarely overfits, proposing that successful strategies are highly compressible. The authors test this by creating "research agents" that either compress the agent's output (reproducing performance with minimal information) or compress the agent's input (using only binary feedback). Their findings suggest that these compression bottlenecks have minimal impact on performance across diverse ML tasks, supporting the compressibility hypothesis.
top_papers.figure_caption: Compressibility of an autonomous agent’s language model pre-training strategy. An explorer agent develops a 12-layer, 768-dimensional GPT with ∼ 30 {\( \sim \)}30 non-default choices; we compress its strategy into progressively shorter prompts (64 down to 4 tokens) and hand each to 5 independent reproducers that implement the strategy without the explorer’s code or validation set. Left : compressed prompts. Tokens encode architecture ( 12L d768 ), optimizer ( Muon .1 ), activation ( ReLU 2 ), batch size ( b2M ), and normalization ( QKnorm ); color indicates survival depth (dark blue = survives tightest budgets, red = dropped first). Right : reproducer holdout loss (BPB); dot = mean, gray band = min–max, dashed line = explorer before compression. Performance holds to 16 tokens; a cliff at 8 tokens coincides with loss of batch size, MLP ratio, and QK-norm.

top_papers.title: When to Align, When to Predict: A Phase Diagram for Multimodal Learning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a unified linear framework to understand when cross-modal alignment (CA) and cross-modal prediction (CP) are effective for multimodal learning. It derives separation ratios showing that alignment fails with strong nuisance correlation, while prediction succeeds based on source modality quality. This work provides a phase diagram to diagnose underperformance in multimodal tasks, particularly in scientific domains.

top_papers.title: Data Journalist Agent: Transforming Data into Verifiable Multimodal Stories
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Data2Story, a multi-agent framework that acts as a virtual data journalism newsroom. Its core method orchestrates specialized agents to transform raw data into verifiable, multimodal stories. The key contributions are ensuring all claims are evidence-grounded through an "Inspector" agent and generating articles with diverse modalities like interactive maps, beyond just text and static charts.
top_papers.figure_caption: Data2Story turns a raw dataset ( e.g., a CSV) into a verifiable, multimodal article ( i.e., a website). We use a “Pick a card” dataset as an illustration. This transformation involves information seeking ( e.g., “why the Ace of Spades dominates human choice?”), data analysis via programming ( e.g., computing card-selection distributions across demographics), narrative storytelling ( e.g., weaving cultural and psychological explanations into a cohesive article), and multimodal design ( e.g., an interactive card-drawing demo).

top_papers.title: The Shibboleth Effect: Auditing the Cross-Lingual Distributional Skew of Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the "Shibboleth Effect," a cross-lingual distributional skew in LLMs, by using a geopolitical wargame where models play in English or Turkish. The core contribution is demonstrating that LLMs exhibit different behavioral tendencies, like increased coercive rhetoric, when operating in languages other than English, highlighting a significant bias in their cross-lingual capabilities.

top_papers.title: VISTA: A Versatile Interactive User Simulation Toolkit for Agent Evaluation
top_papers.abstract: VISTA is a novel toolkit designed to address the limitations of current interactive agent evaluation methods. Its core method involves a versatile simulation framework that supports both UI and API actions, allowing for more realistic user behavior modeling. VISTA's key contribution is its suite of six metrics, which enable comprehensive evaluation of simulated interactions, ensuring thorough exploration of agent capabilities and failure modes.
top_papers.figure_caption: An overview of VISTA . It configures each task scenario with a user profile, user goal, and relevant domain knowledge. During interaction, the user agent observes the webpage and decides whether to take UI actions directly or invoke user-side API tools for additional task-grounded information. The resulting interactions are then evaluated in terms of capability coverage, utterance realism, and interaction effectiveness, measured by agent failures.

top_papers.title: A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery, moving beyond just search and execution. Its core method introduces "model formation" through qualitative reasoning as the crucial, yet underdeveloped, middle layer. This layer enables AI to identify structural inadequacies in current models and reformulate them by understanding what's missing, thereby facilitating true scientific insight.

top_papers.title: AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), a novel method for evaluating AI agents. AAA uses judge agents and standardized protocols (A2A and MCP) for all interactions, creating a unified, agent-agnostic framework. This approach enhances openness, standardization, and reproducibility in agent evaluation by separating assessment logic from agent implementation and enabling fair comparisons across diverse agent designs.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Comparison between Traditional LLM/Agent benchmarks and AAA. AAA reduces the number of integrations from N × M N\( \times \) M to N + M N+M , while completely separating the benchmark and target agent as shown in the gray boxes.

top_papers.title: EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis
top_papers.abstract: EpiBench is a verifiable benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents' ability to perform short-horizon epigenomics analysis. It assesses if agents can make sound decisions from realistic workflow states and produce deterministically gradable answers across various epigenomics assays. The paper's contribution lies in demonstrating that current AI agents, even advanced models, struggle to consistently pass these evaluations, often failing at deeper, assay-specific reasoning despite finding correct files and generating useful intermediate results.

top_papers.title: SpatialClaw: Rethinking Action Interface for Agentic Spatial Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: SpatialClaw addresses the limitations of existing spatial reasoning agents by proposing a novel action interface that uses code. This training-free framework allows VLMs to flexibly compose and tailor operations for complex 3D/4D spatial reasoning, overcoming the constraints of single-pass execution or rigid structured tool calls.

top_papers.title: HyperTool: Beyond Step-Wise Tool Calls for Tool-Augmented Agents
top_papers.abstract: HyperTool addresses the inefficiency of step-wise tool calls in LLM agents by introducing a unified, MCP-style interface. This allows models to execute complex tool workflows within a single, code-block-based call, abstracting away low-level dataflow and reducing context consumption. This novel approach significantly improves agent performance on cross-tool compositional tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of context management paradigms in tool-augmented agents. (a) Step-wise execution expands atomic calls and observations into the trace. (b) Trace-level compression shortens the trace after expansion. (c) HyperTool manages context at execution time by folding dependent tool operations into one executable block and returning only the task-relevant result.

top_papers.title: Influcoder: Distilling Decoders' Gradient Influence Rankings into an Encoder for Data Attribution
top_papers.abstract: Influcoder distills the gradient influence of individual training samples on an LLM's decoder into a compact encoder. This allows for efficient and scalable data attribution, enabling researchers to quickly identify which training data points are responsible for specific model behaviors, such as toxicity.
top_papers.figure_caption: Schema for the training pipeline of the Influcoder. Stage 1: full pool and query samples are passed to the target decoder model, then we compute LoRA gradients on the responses only. Finally we project the gradients with CountSketch and compute ground truth cosine similarity influences. Stage 2: full pool and query samples are passed through the encoder to produce influence embeddings, cosine similarity is taken to generate influence predictions.

top_papers.title: Abstracting Cross-Domain Action Sequences into Interpretable Workflows
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces WorkflowView, a framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to transform granular user interaction logs into interpretable, high-level workflows. This approach addresses limitations of prior deep learning methods by offering greater robustness to noise and improved generalization across different applications and tasks. WorkflowView's core contribution lies in its ability to extract meaningful insights from complex user behavior, enabling better understanding and improvement of digital products.
top_papers.figure_caption: We propose an LLM-based framework for hierarchical abstraction of user action sequences into interpretable high-level activities (WorkflowView). The left panel illustrates raw action sequences from three domains. WorkflowView enables downstream inferences with task-specific steerability, such as reconstructing user intent in browsers, predicting student dropout in MOOCs, and privacy-preserving categorization of document-centric workflows.

top_papers.title: StreamMemBench: Streaming Evaluation of Agent Memory for Future-Oriented Assistance
top_papers.abstract: StreamMemBench is a novel benchmark designed to evaluate how well AI agents can use past observations and interactions to provide future-oriented assistance. It achieves this by creating two-step task sequences where an initial task tests evidence utilization, and a subsequent task assesses the reuse of feedback and interaction experience. This approach addresses a gap in existing benchmarks by testing the entire memory trajectory, revealing that current systems struggle to effectively leverage observed evidence.

top_papers.title: AgentSpec: Understanding Embodied Agent Scaffolds Through Controlled Composition
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces AgentSpec, a framework for understanding and comparing embodied LLM agents. AgentSpec standardizes the interfaces of various agent components (like reasoning, memory, and action) allowing them to be easily swapped and recombined. This modular approach enables controlled experiments to analyze the specific contributions of different modules and model backbones to agent performance.
top_papers.figure_caption: AgentSpec turns tightly coupled embodied-agent pipelines into a controlled modular design space with fixed typed interfaces, enabling systematic module composition and revealing interaction effects between reasoning, memory, reflection, action, and learning.

top_papers.title: Bayesian Inference and Decision Audits for Public Archives of Frontier AI Evaluations
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a Bayesian inference framework to analyze public AI evaluation archives, recognizing they are selective time series rather than terminal leaderboards. Its core contribution is demonstrating how different historical data interpretations, even with the same terminal model, lead to significantly different conclusions about AI progress timelines. This highlights the need for decision audits to account for these observational uncertainties.

top_papers.title: Consensus-based Agentic Large Language Model Framework for Harmonized Tariff Schedule Code Classification
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a consensus-based agentic LLM framework for classifying Canadian 10-digit Harmonized Tariff Schedule (HTS) codes. The core method involves multiple agents retrieving information, performing semantic searches on official documents, and using evidence-grounded reasoning. The key contribution is a novel consensus mechanism that validates classifications through element-wise voting and confidence estimation, with human escalation for complex cases, aiming to improve accuracy in maritime logistics.
top_papers.figure_caption: Architecture of the proposed HTS classification framework, showing the interaction between retrieval, reasoning, validation, and user clarification modules.

top_papers.title: Phantoms and Disclosures: a Causal Framework for Auditing Synthetic Data
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes a causal auditing framework to detect and explain private information disclosures in synthetic data generated by AI models. It distinguishes between "true disclosures" (direct reproduction) and "phantom disclosures" (incidental generation) by using statistical hypothesis testing on partitioned real data, without requiring model access. This allows for rigorous privacy auditing against baselines like zero-learning or Differential Privacy.

top_papers.title: RAID: Semantic Graph Diffusion for True Cold-Start and Cross-Lingual Forecasting
top_papers.abstract: RAID addresses the challenge of forecasting for new items with no historical data (true cold-start). It leverages textual metadata to retrieve semantically similar items and uses this information to guide a diffusion model for forecasting. This approach enables accurate predictions and interval coverage for unseen items, outperforming existing methods and reducing inference time.
top_papers.figure_caption: The RAID Architecture. (Top) Inductive Semantic Graph: Metadata is mapped to a shared semantic manifold via BGE-M3 to construct a sparse retrieval graph. (Bottom Left) Shape-Scale Decomposition: Neighbor trajectories are normalized into scale-invariant shapes and aggregated; a target-specific scale is predicted via MLP to reconstruct a deterministic base forecast \( \mu \)_{\( \text{base} \)} . (Bottom Right) Gated Diffusion: A Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models the residual uncertainty 𝐫 \( \mathbf{r} \) , conditioned on the graph context 𝐜 \( \mathbf{c} \) via Adaptive Layer Norm. The final forecast y ^ = μ base + g ⋅ r ^ 0 \( \hat{y} \)=\( \mu \)_{\( \text{base} \)}+g\( \cdot \)\( \hat{r}_{0} \) is emitted as a probabilistic Horizon- H H distribution.

top_papers.title: A Multi-Center Benchmark for Abdominal Disease Diagnosis and Report Generation from Non-Contrast CT
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel multi-center benchmark and dataset for diagnosing abdominal diseases and generating radiology reports using only non-contrast CT (NCCT). The core method learns to infer contrast-enhanced findings from NCCT, addressing risks and burdens associated with contrast agents. The contribution lies in demonstrating that NCCT alone contains sufficient diagnostic information and in providing a robust evaluation framework for deep learning models in this domain.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the benchmark workflow.

top_papers.title: Exploring Extrinsic and Intrinsic Properties for Effective Reasoning with Code Interpreter
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how to improve Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning with Code Interpreters (CIs) by analyzing "extrinsic" (important tokens) and "intrinsic" (cognitive behaviors like verification and backtracking) properties. The core method involves identifying and quantifying these properties in effective CI reasoning. The main contribution is demonstrating that these properties are crucial for strong performance and can be leveraged during both inference (by adding tokens) and training to enhance LLM reasoning capabilities.

top_papers.title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper red-teams Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 LLMs against automated jailbreak attacks. The core method involves generating millions of adversarial attempts and having judge models verify successes. The key contribution is demonstrating that while these models are robust, adaptive iterative attacks, particularly tree-of-attacks, can still break Opus 4.8 significantly more often than Fable 5, highlighting the limitations of aggregate robustness metrics.

top_papers.title: Learning Cardiac Electrophysiology Digital Twins Through Agentic Discovery of Hybrid Structure
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces LEADS, a framework that uses an LLM agent to automatically discover personalized cardiac electrophysiology digital twin models. LEADS formulates domain knowledge as an action space, allowing the agent to iteratively select, combine, and refine hybrid physics-neural architectures. This approach overcomes the limitations of manual model design and LLM-only methods by ensuring physically grounded and interpretable models for stable cardiac simulations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of LEADS. Left: the LLM agent follows an Observe-Think-Act loop to iteratively assemble and refine hybrid cardiac EP models. Right: the structured action space with a neural diffusion catalog and a physics-based reaction catalog.

top_papers.title: The Measurement Gap in the Automation of EU Law: Benchmarking Doctrinal Legal Reasoning under the EU AI Act
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses a critical "measurement gap" in evaluating AI for legal tasks. It argues that current benchmarks focus on paralegal work, failing to assess AI's ability to perform "doctrinal legal reasoning," the core of legal interpretation. The authors propose developing a benchmark for this specific capability, which is essential for the EU AI Act's requirement of "appropriate accuracy" in judicial AI.

top_papers.title: WEQA: Wearable hEalth Question Answering with Query-Adaptive Agentic Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: WEQA addresses the challenge of answering questions about wearable health data by employing a query-adaptive agent framework. It unifies Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning with specialized wearable data analysis tools. An LLM controller dynamically synthesizes execution plans, routes queries to appropriate analytical and modeling tools, and audits responses with external knowledge, creating a more effective approach for this complex data type.
top_papers.figure_caption: Examples of heterogeneous wearable health questions and the computational requirements. The right panel denotes representative computation steps, such as temporal analysis and model prediction.

top_papers.title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces TAC, the first agentic benchmark to evaluate AI's ability to avoid animal exploitation in real-world actions like travel booking. The core method involves presenting AI agents with scenarios where they must choose options, and TAC measures their success in avoiding exploitative activities. The key contribution is demonstrating that current frontier AI models, even when asked directly, fail to avoid animal exploitation when acting as agents, highlighting a critical gap in their welfare reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Base welfare rate across seven frontier models (higher bar = model more often selected the safe option), with Wilson 95% confidence intervals ( N = 144 N=144 per model). Stars indicate the two-sided exact binomial test against the chance level p 0 = 0.64 p_{0}=0.64 : ∗ p < 0.05 *\ p<0.05 , ∗ ∗ p < 0.01 **\ p<0.01 , ∗ ∗ ∗ p < 0.001 ***\ p<0.001 , ns = not significant. All bars fall below chance. Completion rates are 100% across all models.

top_papers.title: Evaluating Open-Source LLMs for Multi-Label ATT&CK Technique Classification on CTI Reports
top_papers.abstract: This paper evaluates open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) for classifying multi-label MITRE ATT&CK techniques in complex Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports. The core method involves creating a new, realistic dataset of human-annotated sentences from CTI reports to overcome the limitations of existing simplified benchmarks. The main contribution is providing an accurate baseline performance of LLMs on this challenging, real-world task, revealing their current capabilities and limitations.

top_papers.title: Learning from the Self-future: On-policy Self-distillation for dLLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces d-OPSD, the first on-policy self-distillation method for diffusion LLMs (dLLMs). It uniquely trains dLLMs by using self-generated answers as suffix conditioning and applying step-level supervision, aligning with the iterative denoising process. This approach allows dLLMs to learn from their "self future-experience" and achieves superior performance on reasoning tasks compared to existing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: The reasoning performance and sample efficiency comparisons between the RLVR baseline (diffu-GRPO (Zhao et al. , 2025 ) ) and our approach, d-OPSD.

top_papers.title: Unintended Effects of Geographic Conditioning in Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how LLMs unintentionally leak geographic information when conditioned on user location metadata. The core method involves evaluating "location leakage" by prompting models with geographically neutral inputs and observing the frequency of region-specific outputs. The key contribution is the discovery of a "structural conditioning effect," showing that even a placeholder for location in the user profile can significantly increase geographic leakage, highlighting a previously unrecognized bias in how LLMs process contextual information.
top_papers.figure_caption: Injecting a location-specific user profile shifts model output from generic to geographically grounded, even when the user prompt is location-agnostic.

top_papers.title: Beyond Safe Data: Pretraining-Stage Alignment with Regular Safety Reflection
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes "Safety Reflection Pretraining" to improve LLM safety by integrating self-monitoring directly into the pretraining process. Instead of just filtering unsafe data, it inserts short "safety reflections" into the training corpus. This method establishes a foundational safety capability that enhances resistance to attacks and improves safety classification.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Safety Reflection Pretraining (SRP) . The top row and the bottom-left example illustrate our data pipeline on the pretraining corpus: we split each text into segments and insert a short safety reflection after each segment, so that the model learns to regularly judge the safety of its generation. The bottom-right bar chart reports the attack success rate (ASR, lower is better) of SRP against standard pretraining, where SRP performs best on every metric. GCG is the average ASR over two benchmarks (AdvBench and DirectHarm4); Benign denotes benign finetuning (on GSM8K) and Adversarial denotes adversarial finetuning (on Harmful HEx-PHI).

top_papers.title: Data Intelligence Agents: Interpreting, Modeling, and Querying Enterprise Data via Autonomous Coding Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Data Intelligence Agents (DIA), a system that uses autonomous coding agents to streamline enterprise data integration. DIA's core method involves agents that generate, execute, and validate data artifacts, leveraging shared memory for efficiency. Its main contribution is demonstrating that this execution-grounded, agent-based approach significantly improves data querying performance and generalizes across various SQL benchmarks.
top_papers.figure_caption: DIA against the best prior system on each of the seven SQL benchmarks, ordered by margin. Each benchmark is scored by its official metric.

top_papers.title: Language Models as Interfaces, Not Oracles: A Hybrid LLM-ML System for Pediatric Appendicitis
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ClaMPAPP, a hybrid system that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as an interface to extract structured clinical features from free-text notes, rather than as a direct diagnostic tool. These extracted features are then validated and fed into a traditional machine learning model (XGBoost) for more stable risk prediction of pediatric appendicitis. The core contribution is a robust system that combines the accessibility of LLMs with the reliability of structured ML for improved clinical decision support.
top_papers.figure_caption: ClaMPAPP Hybrid System Architecture. The end-to-end pipeline consists of four main stages: (1) Unstructured clinical notes serve as the input. (2) The ClaMPAPP Interface Layer uses an LLM for feature extraction, converting free text into a structured JSON output. (3) A Safety Validator acts as a quality gate, performing range and type checks. Invalid data is either handled automatically(e.g., converted to NaN) or triggers a query back to the clinician. (4) The validated feature vector is passed to the XGBoost model for a final risk prediction and diagnosis.

top_papers.title: Trade-offs in Medical LLM Adaptation: An Empirical Study in French QA
top_papers.abstract: This paper empirically studies trade-offs in adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) for French medical question answering. It compares continual pretraining (CPT) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT), finding that CPT+SFT is often best for multiple-choice QA, but SFT alone is a cost-effective default due to small, often insignificant gains from CPT. For open-ended QA, CPT improves overlap metrics while SFT can degrade generation quality.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the experimental pipeline for evaluating medical domain adaptation strategies.

top_papers.title: X+Slides: Benchmarking Audience-Conditioned Slide Generation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces X+Slides, a novel benchmark for evaluating audience-conditioned slide generation. Its core method involves a dynamic evaluation framework that assigns audience-specific utility weights to source-grounded probes. The main contribution is addressing the critical gap in existing benchmarks by explicitly considering the target audience's needs, leading to more realistic and effective slide generation systems.

top_papers.title: Diffusion-Proof: Recipe for Formal Theorem Proving Beyond Auto-Regressive Generation
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Diffusion-Proof**, the first framework for training and applying diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) to formal theorem proving. It addresses limitations of auto-regressive models by leveraging dLLMs' iterative denoising generation, which is better suited for maintaining long-range coherence critical in formal mathematics. This work pioneers the use of dLLMs for this complex reasoning task.
top_papers.figure_caption: Diffusion-Proof framework: (a) Data Collection and Model Training: We first collect a 5.5M Lean SFT dataset from previous works, then perform cleaning and selection to obtain 300k natural language (NL)-formal language (FL) mixture data to fine-tune Fast-dLLM-V2-7B into dLLM-Prover-7B . Subsequently, we select data with subgoal decomposition and perform block filling to formulate data for corrector training. The dLLM-Corrector-7B is fine-tuned by the dataset using Large-block correction training. (b) Proof Writing & Correction: The prover model firstly generates the complete proof for the Lean4 theorems using block-diffusion generation. If the verification fails but the skeleton is correct, the corrector model applies large diffusion block correction to adjust the subgoal proofs based on bi-directional information.

top_papers.title: Native Active Perception as Reasoning for Omni-Modal Understanding
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OmniAgent, a novel approach to video understanding that treats it as an active reasoning process rather than passive frame-by-frame processing. OmniAgent uses an iterative Observation-Thought-Action cycle, inspired by POMDPs, to selectively extract relevant audio-visual information into a textual memory, thus decoupling computational cost from video length. Its key contributions are methods for bootstrapping this active perception through supervised fine-tuning and a reinforcement learning technique that adapts to conversational turns.
top_papers.figure_caption: The OmniAgent Framework for Native Active Perception. Unlike passive methods that process video frames uniformly, OmniAgent treats perception as an iterative reasoning process via an Observation-Thought-Action (OTA) cycle. Conditioned on a specific query, the agent executes on-demand actions to selectively gather audio-visual cues, distilling high-dimensional transient percepts into a persistent textual memory until sufficient evidence is gathered to produce the final answer.

top_papers.title: Analyzing Defensive Misdirection Against Model-Guided Automated Attacks on Agentic AI Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper analyzes how automated, model-guided attacks can overcome traditional defenses against agentic AI systems. The core method uses a probabilistic model to show that predictable defenses provide feedback to attackers, leading to high success rates. The key contribution is proposing "detect-and-misdirect" defenses, which use controlled, misleading responses to trick the attacker's automated judge and reduce their attack effectiveness.
top_papers.figure_caption: Example of contextual misdirection response generated by CMPE method in Algorithm 2 against an automated jailbreak request.

top_papers.title: Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces "Contagion Networks," a framework to quantify how evaluator biases spread among interacting LLM agents. The core method involves measuring the "Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix" to track bias propagation. The key contribution is demonstrating that even within the same LLM, evaluator biases consistently spread, and that larger evaluator committees can significantly suppress this contagion.
top_papers.figure_caption: Cross-agent contagion network \( \Gamma_{3} \) (mean over n = 2 n=2 seeds). All edges are dashed ( γ < 1.0 \( \gamma \)<1.0 ) indicating the suppression regime for the chain topology. The spectral radius ρ ¯ ​ ( Γ 3 ) = 1.402 ± 0.003 \( \bar{\rho} \)(\( \Gamma_{3} \))=1.402\( \pm \) 0.003 applies to the fully-connected topology; under chain propagation, all link-level coefficients remain below 1.0, satisfying Corollary 1.

top_papers.title: How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates the reasoning transparency of DiffusionGemma, a large language model that operates in a continuous latent space. The authors decompose transparency into variable and algorithmic components, finding that while DiffusionGemma initially appears less transparent due to its computational structure, they can map its internal information flow through an interpretable token bottleneck, suggesting a path to understanding its reasoning process.
top_papers.figure_caption: A simplified architecture diagram of DiffusionGemma, with the first two denoising steps shown; see Section ˜ 2.1 for an in-depth description of DiffusionGemma’s architecture and sampling. We additionally show the path with the largest opaque serial depth with and without the assumption that the intermediates 𝒔 \( \boldsymbol{s} \) are interpretable; see Section ˜ 2.2 for more details on this calculation.

top_papers.title: LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents
top_papers.abstract: LedgerAgent introduces a novel method for tool-calling agents by maintaining a structured "ledger" of task states separately from the prompt. This ledger stores relevant facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions, preventing agents from relying on stale or incomplete information. The core contribution is improved policy adherence and more robust state management, mitigating common failure modes in customer-service agent interactions.
top_papers.figure_caption: A standard agent retrieves a reservation record but later issues a policy-violating cancellation because the relevant state remains implicit in the prompt. LedgerAgent stores successful tool returns in a schema-anchored ledger and checks proposed environment-changing calls against this observed state before execution. In this example, the stored reservation state allows the gate to block the cancellation and return policy-grounded feedback.

top_papers.title: StylisticBias: A Few Human Visual Cues Drive Most Social Biases in MLLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces StylisticBias, a benchmark that isolates the impact of individual visual attributes on social biases in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). By generating controlled variations of faces, the study reveals that specific visual cues like fashion style, rather than just group identity, significantly influence MLLM judgments. The core contribution is identifying these key stylistic attributes that drive a large portion of social biases in MLLMs.
top_papers.figure_caption: Benchmark construction and evaluation. (1) Benchmark Generation: A Cartesian product of four demographic attributes yields 90 configurations from which 500 synthetic base faces 𝒳 b \( \mathcal{X}_{b} \) are generated. Each base face receives ∼ \( \sim \) 50 single-attribute variations, yielding ∼ \( \sim \) 25,000 images 𝒳 v = { v ​ ( x ) ∣ x ∈ 𝒳 b } \( \mathcal{X}_{v} \)=\{v(x)\( \mid \) x\( \in \)\( \mathcal{X}_{b} \)\} . (2) Benchmark Evaluation: Six MLLMs perform binary forced-choice judgments across 25 scenarios under 3 seeds and 4 prompt orderings. The prediction shift Δ i ​ ( x v ) = φ i ​ ( x v ) − φ i ​ ( x b ) \( \Delta_{i} \)(x_{v})=\( \varphi_{i} \)(x_{v})-\( \varphi_{i} \)(x_{b}) quantifies how strongly each visual attribute moves model judgment.

top_papers.title: Causal Discovery in the Era of Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that AI agents in causal discovery should assist the workflow by inspecting data, explaining methods, and clarifying outputs, rather than directly supplying causal relationships. Their core contribution is the principle that causal claims must remain grounded in data, explicit assumptions, and formal algorithms, with agents acting as facilitators. This approach aims to ensure transparency and prevent LLM-generated artifacts from masquerading as data-driven causal evidence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Framework of causal-learn+ . Agents support data analysis, preprocessing, algorithm recommendation, expert-knowledge incorporation and interpretation, while formal causal discovery remains tied to provably-correct algorithms, explicit assumptions and user-approved choices.

top_papers.title: Kamera: Unified Position-Invariant Multimodal KV Cache for Training-Free Reuse
top_papers.abstract: Kamera proposes a unified KV cache mechanism that enables training-free reuse of multimodal context. Its core method involves storing a low-rank conditioning patch alongside the standard KV cache, which captures cross-chunk dependencies lost in naive reuse. This allows for efficient multi-hop reasoning by recovering crucial information that standard caches miss, significantly improving accuracy without requiring any model retraining.
top_papers.figure_caption: Three reuse patterns beyond the window, and what each costs. Top (prefix/radix): cached vision embeddings still miss whenever reuse sits at a shifted position (reorder, slide, look-back), so the LLM prefill re-runs. Bottom (Kamera): chunks stored position-free; reorder reuses one orbit-patch, a survivor relocates for free ( R ​ ( δ ) R(\( \delta \)) , no patch), and a recalled chunk is rehydrated by a patch on its fixed earlier context. Survivors cost a rotation; only recall costs a patch.

top_papers.title: The Topology of Ill-Posed Questions: Persistent Homology for Detection and Steering in LLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes using persistent homology to analyze the internal states of Large Language Models (LLMs) when processing ill-posed questions. By modeling transformer layer hidden states as point clouds and summarizing their topological features, the authors create a "topology representation" of the question. This representation can then be used to steer LLM response behavior, offering a unified approach to handling various types of problematic queries.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of our topology-based framework. We collect prompt-token hidden states across layers, treat each layer as a token point cloud, compute finite H 0 H_{0} persistence features, and use the resulting topology vectors for ill-posedness classification and topology-conditioned activation steering.

top_papers.title: VeriEvol: Scaling Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning via Verifiable Evol-Instruct
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces VeriEvol, a framework for scaling multimodal mathematical reasoning by focusing on verifiable data construction. It decouples prompt difficulty, increased through route-specific evolution operators, from answer reliability, enforced by offline hypothesis-testing falsification. VeriEvol iteratively generates harder, image-grounded prompts and verifies answers using a multi-source counter-evidence approach, ensuring data volume and reliability without relying on potentially flawed human labels.

top_papers.title: On the Limits of Prompt-Conditioned Language Models as General-Purpose Learners
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that language's inherent limitations as a communication channel create an "expressivity floor" for prompt-conditioned LLMs. Even with perfect inference and execution, complex tasks exceeding language's capacity become indistinguishable, leading to unavoidable errors. The core contribution is a theoretical framework, using a cheap-talk game and PAC-Bayes bounds, to demonstrate this fundamental limitation on LLMs' general-purpose learning capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Conceptual Diagram

top_papers.title: SVD-Surgeon: Optimal Singular-Value Surgery for Large Language Model Compression
top_papers.abstract: SVD-Surgeon applies the Optimal Brain Surgeon framework to singular values for LLM compression. It computes optimal updates to retained singular values to minimize loss after pruning, and identifies which singular values are most important to prune.
